Defendants William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl, Paul Wattis, Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation'S Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
Off Calendar May-14-2019 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar May-14-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS WILLIAM BARLOW, LESLIE BERRIMAN, PHILLIP BOWLES, WILLIAM BUICE, KEVIN KING, HELYN MACLEAN, WILHELM OEHL, PAUL WATTIS, GRABHORN INSTITUTE, LYRA CORPORATION'S Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint.

Defendants Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation, William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl and Paul Wattis' motion to strike portions of the first amended complaint is denied.

The notice of motion is improperly directed at portions of Plaintiff's original complaint, which has been superseded by the first amended complaint. Defendants' various arguments lack merit. Defendants' contention that punitive damages are unavailable because this action is "really based on the alleged breach of contract in the promissory notes" (Dem. at 5) is groundless, because a promissory note or other written contract may be an instrument by which a defendant engages in "financial abuse" of an elder within the meaning of the Elder Abuse Act. The Act specifically provides that financial abuse may be effected "by means of an agreement." (Welf. & Inst. Code 15610.30(a)1),(c); see, e.g., Mahan v. Charles W. Chan Ins. Agency, Inc. (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 841, 861-862 [complaint that alleged defendants arranged for restructuring of life insurance policies that resulted in more limited coverage at greater cost adequately alleged deprivation of property under Act].) Plaintiff's allegations that Defendants induced him to make multiple cash advances to them between September 28, 2010 and December 13, 2007, with the knowledge that those advances represented the only monies he had to fund his retirement and that they were unlikely to be repaid (FAC 32-34) are sufficient to allege that Defendants acted with recklessness, oppression, fraud, or malice. Defendants assert that Welf. & Inst. Code 15657.5 applies only to conduct occurring after its effective date, but the first amended complaint expressly disclaims any liability on Defendants' part predating September 28, 2010, when Plaintiff allegedly made the first cash advance. (FAC 25.) Defendants' argument "There Is No Elder Abuse Act Claim Available to Hoyem As A Matter Of Law" is misplaced, as Defendants have not demurred to the second cause of action, and in any event again is improperly based on extrinsic matters outside the proper scope of demurrer.

Defendants' motion to strike the allegations that he was "threatened by Defendants with a LYRA bankruptcy" in which, as a result of how the promissory notes were structured, he would be relegated to the status of an equity security holder and likely would not be entitled to any distributions (FAC 28, 31, 34) is denied. Those allegations are relevant to Plaintiff's Elder Abuse Act claim, which requires a showing that Defendants deprived Plaintiff of a property interest for a wrongful use or with intent to defraud, and for that reason are not barred by the litigation privilege. (See Oren Royal Oaks Venture v. Greenberg, Bernhard, Weiss & Karma, Inc. (1988) 42 Cal.3d 1157, 1168 ["when allegations of misconduct properly put an individual's intent at issue in a civil action, statements made during the course of a judicial proceeding may be used for evidentiary purposes in determining whether the individual acted with the requisite intent."].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-14-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS WILLIAM BARLOW, LESLIE BERRIMAN, PHILLIP BOWLES, WILLIAM BUICE, KEVIN KING, HELYN MACLEAN, WILHELM OEHL, PAUL WATTIS, GRABHORN INSTITUTE, LYRA CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendants Grabhorn Institute, Lyra Corporation, William Barlow, Leslie Berriman, Phillip Bowles, William Buice, Kevin King, Helyn Maclean, Wilhelm Oehl and Paul Wattis' demurrer to the first cause of action of the first amended complaint for money and received and the third cause of action for declaratory relief re alter ego/joint agency is overruled.

Defendants' extensive reliance on purported facts not alleged in the first amended complaint is improper on demurrer and cannot render the first amended complaint uncertain or defective. It is elementary that a demurrer can be used only to challenge defects that appear on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See generally California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Guide 2018) 7:8.) The court denies Defendants' request for judicial notice, which seeks notice of matters that are not properly the subject of judicial notice. The court will disregard the Roberts declaration and attached exhibits, which are improper for the same reason.

The first cause of action, in conjunction with the alter ego and agency allegations (FAC 22-23), properly pleads a cause of action for money had and received. (FAC 22-23, 32, 36-41.) "A cause of action for money had and received is stated if it is alleged that the defendant is indebted to the plaintiff in a certain sum for money had and received by the defendant for the use of the plaintiff. The claim is viable wherever one person has received money which belongs to another, and which in equity and good conscience should be paid over to the latter." (Avidor v. Sutter's Place, Inc. (2013) 212 Cal.App.4th 1439, 1454 (citations and internal quotations omitted).) Defendants' arguments raise factual issues that are not properly considered on demurrer.

The third cause of action adequately pleads a cause of action for declaratory relief regarding whether Defendants are alter egos or agents of each other. A general demurrer to a cause of action for declaratory relief must be overruled so long as an actual controversy is alleged; the pleader need not establish it is also entitled to a favorable judgment. (Ludgate Ins. Co. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 592, 606; see also Qualified Patients Ass'n v. City of Anaheim (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 734, 751 [" '[a] general demurrer is usually not an appropriate method for testing the merits of a declaratory relief action . . . .'"].) Contrary to Defendants' contention, a plaintiff alleging an alter ego theory is required to allege only ultimate rather than evidentiary facts. (Rutherford Holdings, LLC v. Plaza Del Rey (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 221, 235-236 [holding that plaintiff made sufficient allegations of alter ego to avoid a demurrer].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/EPS)
